cheatSheets.summary: A practical low-end cheat sheet with chain order, tone moves, level targets, and mix decisions you can adjust later.

steps.name: Space
steps.goal: Use subtle reverb or delay if needed.

steps.name: Limiting
steps.goal: Catch peaks and cap final level.

cards.title: EQ Targets
items: High-pass where possible
Cut boxiness first
Boost for intent, not volume

cards.title: Compression Targets
items: Ratio 3:1 to 4:1
Attack 10-30 ms
Aim 2-6 dB gain reduction

cards.title: Metering Targets
items: Keep peaks below -6 dBFS
Watch low-end buildup
Prioritize Low-end clarity

sections.title: Quick Reminders
items: Leave target peak around -6 dBFS.
Do not normalize at mix stage.

Avoid fixing arrangement issues on bus.

cheatSheets.summary: Set healthy levels from tracks to master, then choose loudness and delivery targets that fit streaming services, physical formats, and the song.

cards.title: Gain Staging Basics
items: Set proper source levels before plugins.
Avoid clipping on tracks, buses, plugins, and the master.
Leave mix bus headroom so mastering can work without rescue moves.

cards.title: LUFS Is a Reference
items: Streaming LUFS numbers are normalization references, not always strict creative targets.
A loud master still gets turned down, but crushed dynamics stay crushed.
Master to what serves the genre, then check how platforms will normalize it.

cards.title: One Main Streaming Master
items: A practical all-platform target is around -14 LUFS integrated.
Use -1.0 dBTP true peak for most services, or -2.0 dBTP for extra codec/Amazon safety.
Keep enough dynamics that the master still punches after normalization.

cards.title: Format-Specific Masters
items: Use a separate vinyl master when pressing records.
Use a separate CD or hi-res master if you want louder or less codec-safe delivery.
Keep the premaster and stems archived so remasters are easy later.

cards.title: True Peak Matters
items: True peak catches inter-sample peaks that regular peak meters can miss.
Lossy codecs can create extra peaks after upload.
Dense, bright, heavily limited masters need more true-peak safety margin.

cards.title: Balance First
items: Fix harshness, boomy lows, and weak vocals before chasing loudness.
If a limiter needs more than a few dB of gain reduction, revisit the mix.
Check quiet playback; strong balances survive lower volume.

rows: Conservative target that gives Amazon and lossy codecs extra headroom.
Normalizes playback; louder masters are turned down.
Sound Check is user-controlled, so the full-level master should still sound good.
Commonly turns loud tracks down and may not boost quiet tracks enough to feel competitive.
Use the stricter true peak ceiling when Amazon or HD delivery matters.
Good translation usually comes from a dynamic master near the general streaming target.
Short-form clips often compete louder, but avoid harsh limiting and codec distortion.
16-bit / 44.1 kHz delivery, often louder than streaming
No playback normalization, so over-limiting is heard exactly as printed
Use dithering when reducing to 16-bit and avoid chasing loudness past the song's comfort zone.
Dedicated cutting master with preserved dynamics
Wide low bass, harsh sibilance, extreme highs, and long sides can distort or skip
Mono or narrow the deep low end and let a vinyl mastering engineer handle final cutting decisions.
Moderate level with controlled highs and lows
Noise floor, hiss, saturation, and dullness if printed too hot or too dark
Leave transient room, avoid brittle top end, and print a test pass if duplicating to tape.
24-bit WAV/AIFF at native session sample rate
Too much limiting defeats the point of a high-resolution release
Keep dynamics and use a slightly higher ceiling such as -0.5 dBTP when no lossy encoding is expected.
Often louder and denser than streaming
Sub buildup, limiter pumping, and harsh highs become obvious on big systems
Check mono low end, kick/bass headroom, and references in the same genre.

cards.title: Vinyl Low End
items: Keep very low bass centered or narrowed.
Avoid huge stereo subs and hard-panned kick/bass information.
Shorter sides allow louder, cleaner cuts than long sides.

cards.title: Vinyl High End
items: De-ess vocals and cymbals before the cutting stage.
Harsh sibilance can distort on playback.
Preserve dynamics so the needle has room to track the groove.

cards.title: Tape Character
items: Tape can add pleasing saturation, but it also raises noise.
Too much low end can blur; too much high end can get brittle or hissy.
Print a reference and listen before duplicating a full run.

sections.title: Delivery Checklist
items: No red clipping on tracks, buses, plugins, or master.
Premaster peaks leave roughly 3-6 dB of headroom.
Integrated LUFS matches the song, genre, and destination.
True peak ceiling is safe for the platform or format.
Streaming master is checked through loudness normalization expectations.
Vinyl, CD, tape, or hi-res releases get separate masters when needed.
Low end, sibilance, mono compatibility, and quiet playback are checked.
Reference against professional releases without copying their loudness blindly.

cheatSheets.summary: A mastering workflow focused on translation, loudness control, and clean delivery.

cards.title: 1. Listen & Analyze
items: Check tonal balance
Check dynamics
Use references

cards.title: 2. EQ
items: Correct broad issues
Avoid over-EQ

cards.title: 3. Dynamics
items: Use light glue
Keep punch

cards.title: 4. Limiter
items: Set final ceiling
Control clipping

sections.title: Final Delivery
items: Check in mono and stereo
Confirm no inter-sample clipping
Export WAV 24-bit (or project spec)
Embed metadata and version naming

cheatSheets.summary: Bring all guitar layers together with bus compression, tone shaping, and controlled stereo image.

cards.title: Purpose
items: Glue rhythm and lead guitars
Create cohesion
Save CPU with shared processing

cards.title: Signal Flow
items: Tracks -> Guitar Bus -> Mix Bus
Process and glue all guitars

cards.title: Level Targets
items: Individual tracks: -18 to -12 dBFS
Bus peaks: -6 dBFS max
Master peak: -1 dBTP ceiling

sections.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
items: Over-compressing and killing dynamics
Over-saturating and losing clarity
Making guitars too wide (phase issues)
Ignoring gain staging and clipping

cheatSheets.summary: Capture clean, intentional performances by controlling the room, choosing simple mic setups, and keeping the session organized from the first take.

cards.title: Pick the Best Room
items: Choose the largest, least boxy room you can use.
Avoid bare parallel walls, glass, tile, and sharp reflective corners near the source.
Put drums and amps away from walls when possible so early reflections are less harsh.

cards.title: Treatment, Not Soundproofing
items: Soundproofing keeps noise in or out and usually needs construction.
Acoustic treatment controls reflections inside the room and is the better home-studio goal.
Treat first reflection points, corners, and the ceiling before buying more microphones.

cards.title: DIY Panels and Bass Traps
items: Build panels from 2-4 inch Rockwool or Owens Corning 703 in simple wood frames.
Use breathable fabric, not plastic, so air and sound can reach the absorber.
Straddle room corners with thicker 4-6 inch traps to reduce low-end buildup.

cards.title: What Works
items: Heavy rugs, curtains, couches, mattresses, bookshelves, and moving blankets can help in the right spots.
A ceiling cloud above drums or the mix position reduces ceiling slap.
Use the mirror trick to find wall spots where monitors or loud sources reflect directly.

cards.title: What Does Not Work
items: Thin foam mostly absorbs high end and does very little for bass problems.
Egg cartons are not useful acoustic treatment.
Covering every surface can make the room dull while leaving low-frequency mud untouched.

cards.title: Vibration Is the Enemy
items: Tighten rattling drum hardware, light fixtures, shelves, doors, and windows before recording.
Decouple amps and speakers from the floor with stands, pads, or dense foam.
Listen for buzzes during loud test passes before the band starts doing real takes.

cards.title: Choose the Right Mic
items: Dynamic mics handle loud sources and reject more room sound.
Condenser mics capture detail on vocals, acoustic guitar, overheads, and rooms.
Ribbon mics can smooth bright guitars or cymbals, but be careful with phantom power.

cards.title: Home-Studio Rule
items: Cardioid patterns are your friend in imperfect rooms.
Aim the back of the mic toward the noisiest bleed source.
Move the mic before reaching for EQ; one inch can change the tone dramatically.

cards.title: Vocals
items: Start 6-8 inches from a cardioid condenser or dynamic mic with a pop filter.
Put absorption behind the singer and behind the mic if the room is bright.
Record a test phrase with the loudest chorus so peaks stay clean.

cards.title: Electric Guitar
items: Start with a dynamic mic near the speaker edge, 1-3 inches from the grille.
Move toward the dust cap for brighter bite or toward the edge for a darker tone.
Turn the amp down if the room is making the mic sound small or harsh.

cards.title: Acoustic Guitar
items: Start around the 12th fret, 8-12 inches back, aimed slightly toward the sound hole.
Avoid pointing straight into the sound hole unless you want boom.
Use one good mic before adding stereo complexity.

cards.title: Phase Checks
items: When two mics hear one source, measure equal distances from the important drum or speaker.
Flip polarity and listen in mono; keep the option that sounds fuller.
If the sound gets hollow, move a mic before recording more takes.

cards.title: Why DI Matters
items: A DI captures a clean, reliable signal before pedals, amps, and room problems.
Keep DI tracks for bass and guitar so you can reamp or use amp sims later.
A clean DI can save a great performance if the amp tone was wrong.

cards.title: Bass
items: Record bass DI on every session.
Blend the DI for low-end solidity with an amp track for grit or air.
Use fresh strings and check tuning often; bass tuning problems are obvious in the mix.

cards.title: Preamps and Levels
items: Use the interface preamp cleanly unless you are intentionally driving color.
At 24-bit, leave headroom instead of recording hot.
Aim loud peaks around -12 to -6 dBFS and avoid clipping anywhere in the chain.

sections.title: Tracking Drums: Choose a Mic Setup

rows: Raw, vintage, balanced by the drummer instead of the mixer
Demos, garage rock, simple songwriting captures
Place the mic 3-6 feet in front of the kit around chest height, then move until kick/snare/cymbals balance.
Focused kit image with solid snare and kick if measured carefully
Small rooms, limited inputs, natural indie or folk drums
Keep both mics the same distance from the snare and kick beater, then check mono before recording.
More low-end control while overheads carry the kit
Rock demos, rehearsal-room sessions, quick live-band tracking
Treat the overheads as the main drum sound and bring the kick mic up only enough to add weight.
Overhead, floor-tom side mic, kick, snare
Open, roomy, classic rock tone with strong kit perspective
Rock, blues, roots, punk, and bands that want live energy
Measure both main mics from the snare center so the snare stays full instead of phasey.
Kick, snare, stereo overheads, one or two tom/room mics
Modern control without too many phase problems
Tune drums first, keep cymbals lower in volume, and use tom mics only if they improve the overhead picture.
Kick, snare top/bottom, toms, hat, overheads, rooms
Polished, editable, punchy, and mix-ready when phase is managed
Professional sessions, dense rock, metal, pop, and productions needing detailed edits
Label every input, check polarity as you add mics, and do a short test mix before committing.
Allows conservative levels without noise problems
Leaves room for surprise hits and singer/drummer excitement
Keeps plugins and preamps in a comfortable operating range
Clipped tracking cannot be repaired cleanly later

cards.title: Start With a Foundation
items: Record a scratch vocal or guide guitar before the rhythm section.
Make sure everyone understands the arrangement before chasing tones.
Keep the guide track if it has feel, but do not let it bleed into final mics.

cards.title: Punches and Comps
items: Record multiple complete takes before punching every small mistake.
Use playlists or take folders so good ideas do not get overwritten.
Punch in a bar early and out a bar late so edits breathe naturally.

cards.title: Keep Tone Consistent
items: Mark mic positions with tape before breaks.
Photograph amp settings, pedal settings, and mic placement.
Retune between takes, especially bass, acoustic guitar, and doubled parts.

cards.title: Headphone Mixes
items: Give each musician enough of themselves, kick/snare, bass, and guide vocal.
Use closed-back headphones to reduce click bleed.
If the take feels stiff, improve the headphone mix before blaming the player.

cards.title: Click or No Click
items: Use a click for tight edits, tempo-based effects, and modern production.
Skip or loosen the click when the song needs natural push and pull.
Tempo-map rehearsals if the band plays best with intentional tempo changes.

cards.title: Session Logistics
items: Name tracks before recording: Kick In, Snare Top, Bass DI, Guitar L, Vocal Lead.
Color-code instruments and route buses before the serious takes.
Keep water, spare strings, drum keys, batteries, picks, and gaff tape nearby.

cards.title: Communication
items: Talk about feel, emotion, and arrangement before talking about gear.
Use short notes after each take so the band stays confident.
Do not over-coach during a great performance.

cards.title: Breaks and Ears
items: Take short breaks before fatigue makes everyone chase bad decisions.
Keep control-room volume moderate so tone choices stay reliable.
Print a rough mix at the end of the day so everyone can review calmly.

cards.title: Backup Routine
items: Save before changing setups or deleting takes.
Back up raw audio before editing or comping.
Keep a dated session copy when the tracking day ends.

sections.title: Tracking Day Checklist
items: Tune drums, guitars, and bass before microphones go up.
Walk the room and remove rattles, buzzes, and loose hardware.
Record a 30-second test and listen in mono for phase problems.
Check the loudest section before setting final preamp gain.
Confirm the headphone mix is comfortable for the player.
Label tracks, inputs, takes, and playlists before the real pass.
Save and back up raw audio before editing.

header.title: Audio Routing
header.subtitle: Buses, sends, inserts, and parallel paths

cheatSheets.summary: Route instruments into clean buses, use sends and inserts intentionally, and build creative parallel paths in Logic Pro.

cards.title: Track
items: The original audio or software instrument channel.
Use inserts here for source-specific cleanup.

cards.title: Instrument Bus
items: Group related tracks: drums, guitars, vocals, keys.
Use the bus fader as one control for that family.

cards.title: Mix / Master Bus
items: All group buses feed the final stereo path.
Use gentle polish, metering, and final level checks here.

cards.title: Simple Map
items: Kick/Snare/Toms/OH -> Drum Bus
Guitars -> Guitar Bus
Vocals -> Vocal Bus
All buses -> Mix Bus -> Stereo Out

rows: Processes 100% of the signal directly on that track or bus.
EQ, compression, gates, de-essing, amp sims, corrective tools.
Use inserts when the sound itself needs to change before it reaches the bus.
Copies part of a signal to an aux through a bus while the original keeps playing.
Reverb, delay, chorus, flanger, parallel compression, cue mixes.
Set time-based FX returns to 100% wet because the dry sound is already on the original track.
Send level follows the track fader.
Most reverbs and delays, where the effect should fade down with the source.
This is Logic's normal choice for shared space effects.
Send level stays independent of the track fader.
Headphone mixes, isolated effects, and some parallel compression setups.
Use carefully so hidden or quiet tracks do not keep feeding the effect unexpectedly.

cards.title: What It Means
items: Split a source into two paths: dry original plus processed copy.
Blend the processed return underneath instead of replacing the original.

cards.title: Why It Works
items: Keeps punch, clarity, and transients from the dry track.
Adds density, sustain, width, grit, or excitement from the parallel path.

cards.title: How to Start
items: Send the source or bus to a new aux.
Compress, saturate, widen, or filter the aux hard.
Pull the aux fader down, then raise it until you miss it when muted.

rows: Drum Bus -> Send -> Aux with fast compressor, saturation, and EQ.
Adds energy and density while the original drums keep their punch.
Vocal or snare -> Send -> Delay Aux -> optional Reverb Aux.
Automate send levels on select words or hits for dramatic echoes without washing out the whole track.
Bass -> Send -> Aux with high-pass EQ, saturation, and compression.
Adds midrange translation on phones/laptops while the clean low-end stays stable.
Lead Vocal -> Sends to short room, slap delay, long delay, and plate reverb auxes.
Lets the vocal feel close, wide, and emotional by blending different spaces independently.

sections.title: Routing Rules of Thumb
items: Use buses to organize related instruments and process families together.
Use inserts when a single track needs tonal or dynamic correction.
Use sends when multiple tracks should share one space or effect.
Keep reverbs and delays on aux returns mostly or fully wet.
Name buses clearly: Drum Bus, Vocal Verb, Slap Delay, Drum Crush.
Check that every bus eventually routes to the mix bus or stereo output.
Mute parallel returns occasionally to confirm they are helping, not cluttering.

header.title: Automation
header.subtitle: Volume rides, movement, and musical control

cheatSheets.summary: Use automation to shape levels, movement, effects, and bleed decisions so the mix feels intentional instead of static.

cards.title: Manual Mix Moves
items: Automation records changes over time: volume, pan, sends, plugin knobs, mutes, and more.
Use it when one static setting works in the verse but fails in the chorus.

cards.title: Why It Matters
items: A good mix moves with the song instead of staying frozen.
Automation can make a vocal feel present, a drum fill explode, or a delay appear only on one word.

cards.title: When to Start
items: Get the rough balance first, then automate.
Do not automate around a bad static mix; fix gain, EQ, and compression first.

cards.title: What Is a Transient?
items: The transient is the first sharp hit of a sound: pick attack, drum stick, consonant, or pluck.
Transients give punch and clarity, but they can also trick meters into showing high peaks.

cards.title: Set Levels by Body + Peak
items: Use peak level to avoid clipping, but use your ear for the body of the sound.
If the transient is loud but the note feels quiet, use compression, clip gain, or a small volume ride.

cards.title: Volume Ride Opinion
items: Ride volume before over-compressing vocals, bass, and solos.
If a word disappears once, automate it. If every word jumps around, use a rider or compressor first.

rows: Bring important moments forward and tuck distractions back.
Lift the lead vocal 1-2 dB in the chorus or raise a guitar lick for one bar.
Add effects only when needed without changing the dry sound.
Send the last vocal word of a phrase to a delay throw.
Make repeats bloom, spin, or disappear musically.
Raise feedback on one transition, then pull it down before the next lyric.
Make effects appear for emphasis but keep the normal mix clear.
Push a chorus, flanger, or distortion wet/dry higher for a bridge.
Create movement, tension, or a reveal.
Low-pass guitars in a verse, then open the filter into the chorus.
Create width or motion without adding more tracks.
Move a synth ear-candy part outward during a fill.
Short room in the verse, longer plate in the chorus.

cards.title: Use Automation When
items: The fix is musical, specific, and intentional.
Examples: one loud syllable, one missing bass note, one drum fill that should leap forward.

cards.title: Use a Rider When
items: The level needs constant small corrections across a whole performance.
Vocal riders and bass riders are great before compression because they feed the compressor more evenly.

cards.title: Use a Gate When
items: You need automatic cleanup between hits or phrases.
Gates work best on close drums, noisy amps, and obvious silence gaps; they work poorly when the bleed is musical.

sections.title: Mic Bleed: Cut It or Keep It?

rows: Cut or reduce bleed with editing, gates, expanders, and fades.
Cleaner close mics make punchy drums, edited guitars, and polished vocals easier to control.
Keep useful bleed if it sounds like room, kit glue, or natural performance energy.
Bleed can make a recording feel like humans playing together instead of isolated parts.
Cut, flip polarity, time-align, or lower the offending mic.
Bad bleed that hollows out the snare, kick, or vocal is not vibe; it is damage.
Cymbal wash in drum close mics
Reduce only as much as needed.
Over-gating toms and snare can make the kit feel fake; use fades or gentle expansion first.
Keep it if it flatters the band, then automate it by section.
Room mics can be low in verses and louder in choruses for size and excitement.

sections.title: Automation Workflow
items: Start with static faders, panning, and gain staging.
Use clip gain for obvious word/note fixes before plugins.
Use riders or compression for constant level control.
Use volume automation for musical emphasis and section changes.
Automate sends, feedback, wet/dry, filters, and reverb time to make transitions pop.
Mute automation lanes occasionally to confirm the song feels worse without them.
Keep bleed when it adds realism; cut it when it causes noise, phase damage, or loss of impact.

header.title: Logic Pro Workflow Cheat Sheet
header.subtitle: Organize, save often, and move fast

cheatSheets.summary: Combined workflow guide covering setup, editing, stack organization, and final export checks.

cards.title: Templates
items: Start from reusable template
Preload routing and markers

cards.title: Folders
items: Song project root
Audio files, backups, stems

cards.title: Saving
items: Save versions frequently
Use Save As intentionally

cards.title: Bouncing
items: Bounce in place for edits
Export WAV 24-bit

cards.title: Templates
items: Create by genre
Save channel strip defaults

cards.title: Folder Setup
items: Create folder tree first
Keep references and stems organized

cards.title: Save Often
items: Use version numbers
Back up project folder

cards.title: Bounce
items: Project or section bounce
Keep export naming consistent

cards.title: Comping
items: Use take folders
Label best takes quickly

cards.title: Punch In/Out
items: Set locators before record
Use cycle when needed

cards.title: Project Management
items: Clean project audio regularly
Archive major milestones

cards.title: Track Stacks
items: Group drums, guitars, vocals
Color-code groups

cards.title: Markers & Locators
items: Label sections quickly
Navigate edits faster

cards.title: Plugin Presets
items: Save reusable chains
Build your go-to palette

sections.title: Final Export Checklist
items: Unused tracks muted or removed
Correct bounce/sample rate settings
Right file format and sample rate
No clipping on printed masters
Correct naming and metadata
Archive project and deliverables

cheatSheets.summary: Quick explanations of common mixing and mastering terms used throughout these sheets.

cards.title: Compressor
items: Turns down loud parts so volume is more controlled.
Can make tracks feel tighter, smoother, and more consistent.

cards.title: Limiter
items: A very strong compressor used at the top end of volume.
Helps stop peaks from clipping and sets a ceiling.

cards.title: Gate
items: Mutes or reduces signal when audio falls below a threshold.
Useful for reducing bleed/noise between drum hits.

cards.title: Normalization
items: Automatically raises audio so the loudest peak hits a chosen level.
Does not improve balance by itself; just changes overall level.

cards.title: High-Pass Filter (HPF)
items: Lets high frequencies pass and cuts low frequencies.
Used to remove rumble and unnecessary low-end.

cards.title: Low-Pass Filter (LPF)
items: Lets low frequencies pass and cuts high frequencies.
Used to tame hiss/harsh top-end or push sounds backward.

cards.title: High Shelf
items: Boosts or cuts all frequencies above a chosen point.
Good for adding air/brightness or reducing harshness.

cards.title: Low Shelf
items: Boosts or cuts all frequencies below a chosen point.
Used for adding weight or cleaning excessive boom.

rows: Loudness Units relative to Full Scale; a perceived loudness measurement over time.
Digital level scale where 0 dBFS is max level before digital clipping.
Two-channel audio (left and right) that creates width and placement.
Single channel centered audio; useful for checking phase and translation.
Placing sounds left, center, or right in the stereo field.
Timing relationship between similar waveforms; poor phase can make sounds thin.
Safety space below 0 dBFS so peaks do not clip.
Distortion from exceeding maximum level; usually harsh and unwanted.
How fast a compressor starts reducing gain after passing threshold.
How fast gain reduction stops after signal falls below threshold.
The level where a dynamic processor starts to act.
How strongly compression is applied once threshold is crossed.
How gently or abruptly compression starts near threshold.
Output gain added after compression to restore loudness.
How much level is currently being turned down.
The short initial hit at the start of a sound.
The held body/tail of a sound after its attack.
Tool that boosts/cuts attack and sustain directly.
Blend of heavily compressed signal with dry signal.
Harmonic coloration that adds density, grit, or warmth.
Extra frequency content above the fundamental pitch.
Generated low-frequency content below the original tone.
A narrow frequency that rings out more than others.
Very narrow EQ cut used to remove a problem tone.
Width of an EQ band: narrow vs broad.
Separate processing of center (mid) and edges (side).
Estimated inter-sample peak that can exceed sample peak.
Average signal energy used as a loudness reference.
How much loudness changes across a song over time.
Difference between quiet and loud parts.
Base level of background hiss or room/system noise.
Low-level noise added when lowering bit depth.
How detailed level values are in digital audio.
Number of audio samples captured each second.
Peaks between samples that can clip in playback.
Professional song used as tonal/loudness comparison.
Shared channel where multiple tracks are grouped.
Copied signal sent to shared effects like reverb.
Channel that receives aux sends and outputs effects.
Processor placed directly in a track signal path.
Send level independent from fader level.
Send level follows track fader changes.
Blend between original and processed signal.
Time-based control of levels, pan, and plugin settings.
Gain change applied to an audio region before inserts.
Setting healthy levels at every stage in the chain.
Input and output level are effectively the same.
How well a stereo mix holds up in mono.
Difference between peaks and average loudness.
Limiter with strict ceiling that blocks overs.
Internal upsampling to reduce digital artifacts.
Using one signal to control another signal’s processor.
Adds harmonics, often to brighten or add presence.
Gentle bus compression to make elements feel cohesive.
One sound hides another in the same range.
EQ/arrangement moves that reduce masking conflicts.
Overall low-mid-high energy distribution in a mix.
Lowest bass region, often around 20-60 Hz.
Bass range that provides weight and power.
Region often tied to body, warmth, and mud.
Upper-mid area affecting clarity and intelligibility.
Very high frequencies that add openness and sheen.
Unpleasant aggressive upper-mid or high frequencies.
Cloudy low-mid buildup that reduces clarity.
Closed, hollow tone usually in low-mids.
Low thumps from consonants like P and B.
Unwanted spill from another source into a mic.
Steps to reduce unwanted mic leakage.
Building one final take from multiple recordings.
Recording over only a selected part.
Small overlap fade that smooths edits.
Exact cut/join location in an audio file.
Compare two signals by phase-cancel checking.
Perceived width and placement of sounds.
Perceived front-to-back distance in a mix.
How close or far a sound feels.
The decaying part of a reverb after source stops.
First room echoes that define space cues.
Time between dry sound and reverb start.
How long reverb takes to fade out.
Natural ambient sound of a recording space.
Perceived environmental space around a source.
How much delayed signal repeats again.
Very short delay used for thickness.
Effect timing locked to song tempo.
Manual tempo entry by tapping rhythm.
Grouped meter view for many channels at once.
Meter mode that keeps peak value visible.
Maximum output level set in limiter/master.
Final stereo channel carrying the full mix.
Render processed audio to a new file.
Export audio from session to file.
Grouped export such as drums, vocals, or music.
Render audio while effects chain stays active.
Reduced accuracy after long loud listening.
How well the mix sounds across different systems.

sections.title: Beginner Rules of Thumb
items: Fix balance first, then process.
Use small EQ/compression moves before big ones.
A/B bypass often at matched loudness.
Check your mix in mono and at low volume.
Leave headroom and avoid clipping on buses.
Trust references and your ears over visuals alone.
